local_names.japanese: 牛ビビンバボウルコンボ

aliases: 牛ビビンバボウルコンボ

japanese: 牛ビビンバボウルコンボ

food_subgroup: Rice bowl dish with meat, vegetables, egg, and fermented condiments

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for beef bibimbap bowl. FOODON_00002809 (rice bowl food product) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Rice dishes) is the closest parent. No specific bibimbap or beef rice bowl code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code found for beef bibimbap bowl combo as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately 550 g, comprising a rice bowl with simmered beef, seasoned vegetables, kimchi, gochujang, and a half-boiled egg, served with miso soup.

nutrient_source.title: Bibimbap (mixed rice with meat and assorted vegetables) — Korean Food Composition Table; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entries for simmered beef, steamed white rice, kimchi, bean sprouts, spinach, egg, and miso soup components
nutrient_source.organization: Rural Development Administration (RDA) Korea / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Bibimbap is a Korean dish served here in a Japanese restaurant context (Sukiya Singapore). Nutrient values are adapted from the Korean Food Composition Table bibimbap entry (RDA Korea) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual components: cooked white rice (FDC ID 168878), simmered beef (FDC ID 174032), kimchi (FDC ID 169999), bean sprouts (FDC ID 169957), spinach (FDC ID 168462), hard/soft-boiled egg (FDC ID 173424), and miso (FDC ID 172950). Values represent a weighted composite per 100 g of the assembled bowl including miso soup, estimated at 550 g total serving weight. Sodium is elevated due to kimchi, gochujang, and miso soup contributions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; supplemented with University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (GI 64) and mixed rice dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef bibimbap bowl combo. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being steamed white rice (GI ~64 per University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by the presence of protein (simmered beef, egg), dietary fibre (bean sprouts, spinach, kimchi), fat, and fermented components (kimchi, gochujang) which are known to attenuate postprandial glycemic response. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 90.75) / 100 ≈ 50.8, where carbohydrate per 550 g serving = 16.5 × 5.5 = 90.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from kimchi, gochujang, miso soup, and seasoned beef
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 2,310 mg per 550 g serving), which approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive patients; requesting reduced miso soup or omitting kimchi may partially lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by white rice, partially attenuated by protein, fibre, and fermented ingredients
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in moderated portions, as the medium GI of approximately 56 and high glycemic load per serving (~50.8) can cause meaningful postprandial blood glucose elevation; pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables or reducing rice portion size is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from simmered beef and egg yolk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious with this dish due to its high sodium content (~2,310 mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat (~5.5 g per serving), both of which are associated with increased cardiovascular risk; choosing a smaller portion and requesting less sauce or miso soup can help reduce sodium and fat load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Bibimbap Bowl Combo is a Japanese-Korean fusion rice bowl dish served at Sukiya, a Japanese gyudon chain restaurant. The bowl features Sukiya's signature simmered beef (gyudon-style thinly sliced beef cooked in a sweet-savory dashi-soy broth) placed over 100% Japan-harvested steamed white rice, accompanied by seasoned bean sprouts and spinach, spicy fermented kimchi, gochujang (a Korean sweet and spicy red pepper paste), and a half-boiled egg with a soft, jammy yolk. The combo is served with miso soup or a drink. The dish combines the umami richness of Japanese simmered beef with the bold, fermented, and spicy flavors of Korean bibimbap tradition. Texturally, it offers soft rice, tender beef, crunchy bean sprouts, wilted spinach, and the creamy richness of a half-boiled egg. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-calorie, protein-rich meal (~118 kcal per 100 g, ~6.8 g protein per 100 g) with a medium glycemic index (~56) driven by white rice but moderated by protein, fibre, and fermented ingredients. The dish is high in sodium (~420 mg per 100 g) due to kimchi, gochujang, miso soup, and seasoned beef, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. It contains common allergens including egg, soy, and wheat. This dish is popular in Singapore as part of Sukiya's menu at locations such as Funan Mall.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Sukiya beef bibimbap?
Is Sukiya bibimbap high in sodium?
What allergens are in beef bibimbap?
What is the glycemic index of bibimbap?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Sukiya Beef Bibimbap Bowl Combo exists in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from the Korean Food Composition Table bibimbap entry and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI moderated by protein, fibre, and fermented ingredients. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data from USDA SR Legacy is well-supported and the adaptation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimate, and the Korean Food Composition Table does not report vitamin D for bibimbap. All other -1 values (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) have been replaced with estimated numeric values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data and are therefore not -1 in this record. The cuisine tag in the input JSON listed 'Japanese Curry' and 'Grilled Chicken Skewers' which are irrelevant to this dish and have been disregarded. The input cuisine field states 'Japanese' which is retained as the restaurant chain (Sukiya) is Japanese, though the dish itself is a Korean-Japanese fusion. Serving size estimated at 550 g based on typical Sukiya bibimbap bowl portion including miso soup. Sodium is notably high due to multiple fermented and seasoned components; this is clinically relevant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. No halal certification is confirmed for Sukiya Singapore. The dish contains egg, soy (miso, gochujang), and wheat (soy sauce in beef seasoning) allergens.
